paper_title: A Simple Theory of Economic Development at the Extensive Industry Margin
research_answer: Yes, occupational proximity to an industry strongly predicts not just entry but also survival in that industry. When a country enters an industry where it already has all the required occupations (distance = 0), there's a 75% chance it remains in that industry in the next period. When it enters an industry at maximum occupational distance, the survival rate falls to around 55%. This 20-percentage-point gap is notable given that the average survival rate across all entrants is about 70%. The implication is that 'stretching' too far beyond existing capabilities may produce fragile, short-lived export positions rather than durable economic diversification.
supporting_quotes: At maximal distance the probability of staying is ∼ 55%. This probability increases to 75% at distance zero. Hence, the observed effect of distance is sizeable.
On average, 70% of entrants are still present in the subsequent period.
